content.claim: Research using panel datasets indicates that early selective tracks (6/8-year gimnázium) are more beneficial for higher-status families, have higher value-added in literacy and mathematics, enroll higher-skilled students, and lead to increased inequality of opportunity, while students left in general tracks experience relative losses.
content.context: Fresh live research through the Legal and governance lens
limitations: Causal weight is disputed among scholars; the mechanism (parental self-selection by status vs. school-driven amplification of advantage) remains contested.

The same research traditions (Kertesi-Kézdi, cited in notes) debate whether the effect is primarily attributable to the tracked structure itself or to supply-side capacity constraints and sorting dynamics across multiple selection points.
content.population: Population described in the cited finding
content.time_scope: As reported by the cited source
